summary: Gruber examines the evidence that Apple is developing a CDMA iPhone 4 (codenamed N92) for Verizon, likely launching in January 2011. He speculates on what opening day demand would look like, arguing it would be a significant event with long lines, especially in cities with poor AT&T coverage. He reports from sources that the CDMA iPhone has reached EVT (engineering verification test) status, placing it on track for a January launch. He frames the broader strategic move through Steve Jobs's own philosophy about the importance of pursuing market share over profits at critical junctures.
key_points: The CDMA iPhone 4 (N92) has reached EVT status, one step below DVT and two below production, consistent with a January 2011 timeline
Gruber estimates Apple could sell at least one million CDMA iPhones on Verizon in the opening weekend, given pent-up demand from Verizon loyalists
Apple's weak spot in secrecy is production — millions of components from suppliers inevitably leak, and signs of CDMA iPhone production are emerging
The CDMA iPhone could serve multiple carriers worldwide, not just Verizon, as Apple moves to maximize market share
Jobs's own critique of 1980s Apple — choosing profits over market share — frames why expanding to CDMA carriers is the strategic imperative now
money_quotes: I think there are going to be lines — especially in places where AT&T service is poor, like New York and San Francisco.
Apple is good at keeping future products secret, and, if anything, they're getting better at it. But the weak spot in the secrecy chain is production.
The CDMA iPhone is no longer a cold storage, keep-it-alive-just-in-case-we-need-it project.
At the critical juncture in the late '80s, when they should have gone for market share, they went for profits.
Take the high end of the market first, establish a brand and presence, then steadily start to expand.
If they're at that juncture with the iPhone now, expansion means CDMA. And in the U.S., it means Verizon.
key_insight: Apple's development of the CDMA iPhone signals it has reached the critical juncture Jobs himself identified — the moment to pivot from high-end exclusivity to aggressive market share expansion.
